original_reasoning: Arabic ghazu = raid/invade; Greek Gaza from Semitic 'azza (strong). Both are Semitic substrate, shared place-name etymology plausible.
Arabic انثنى matches the Greek notion of something twisted or winding.
خوض is entering or wading into something, and χυδαῖος is poured forth abundantly; both meet strongly in the image of fluid spreading and immersion.
Βυσναῖοι denotes the people of Bysna/Beisan, which aligns well with the Arabic gloss naming Beisan as a place in Syria.
Arabic خيشوم and nasal openings align well with Greek chasms as openings; concrete aperture is the common core.
ἀλλάσσω means to change or alter, and the Arabic gloss centers on rows moving from their places and people going off scattered; both share the core of positional change.
Arabic includes a plant gloss and Greek names a plant; species differ, but plant-to-plant is a clear masadiq chain.
Kalaf - skin discoloration/covering vs 'sheath, case, covering' - strong covering/envelope match
